does: This paper introduces MathDuels, a novel evaluation framework that casts language models in dual roles as both problem authors and solvers. Models generate mathematical problems through a three-stage pipeline involving meta-prompting, problem generation, and difficulty amplification, with an independent verifier filtering out ill-posed questions. Each model then solves problems authored by all other participants. The framework applies a Rasch model to jointly estimate solver abilities and problem difficulties, deriving author quality from the difficulty of generated problems. Experiments across 19 frontier models reveal that authoring and solving capabilities are partially decoupled and that this dual-role evaluation uncovers capability distinctions that static benchmarks cannot distinguish.
connects: This work responds to the fundamental problem that frontier language models are reaching saturation on existing mathematical benchmarks like MATH and GSM8K, making it difficult to differentiate state-of-the-art model capabilities. It builds on longstanding approaches to benchmark design and adversarial evaluation, combining them with problem generation research showing that models can create novel mathematical content. The use of the Rasch model connects to psychometric traditions in educational assessment, applying principled measurement theory to estimate both solver ability and problem difficulty jointly—a technique borrowed from standardized testing. The adversarial problem-posing aspect extends work on red-teaming and out-of-distribution robustness evaluation.
enables: This framework opens new directions for evaluating and developing language models beyond static benchmarks by creating adaptive, generative evaluation pipelines. The decoupling of authoring and solving abilities suggests future training methods that deliberately strengthen both capabilities and architectural insights about what makes models effective problem creators versus solvers. The self-play arena structure enables continuous evaluation as new models enter, automatically generating harder problems as the frontier advances—potentially creating a perpetually challenging benchmark immune to ceiling effects. The approach also points toward adversarial problem generation as a tool for robustness testing and could inspire similar dual-role evaluations across other domains like code generation, reasoning, and creative writing.
